Investing in Youth Mental Health with NC Statewide Telepsychiatry Program

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Colleen Briggs
3w
  • Report this post

"There is no health without mental health" - Dr Sy Saeed said it well during our panel today with Dr. Gary Maslow at the NC Chamber Mental Health Summit. They made a compelling case for business leaders to invest in youth mental health to support our future workforce and address rising healthcare costs. I’m proud that the Blue Cross and Blue Shield of North Carolina Foundation is investing $1.5M to expand Dr Saeed's NC Statewide Telepsychiatry Program (NC‑STeP) to the University of North Carolina at Pembroke. When most mental health conditions begin by age 24, we know the importance of getting upstream - investing in prevention and bringing care to places students already know and trust. Delivering services directly on campus helps reduce barriers and ensures support is there when it’s needed. Grateful for our partners at East Carolina University, NC‑STeP, and UNC Pembroke, and for the shared commitment to improving youth mental health across North Carolinia. #NCMentalHealthSummit26

Blue Cross NC

69,200 followers

4w

When mental health care is easier to access, students are better positioned to thrive. The Blue Cross and Blue Shield of North Carolina Foundation is expanding access to mental health care for students at UNC Pembroke through the North Carolina Statewide Telepsychiatry Program. Building on previous work across the UNC System, this partnership will help connect students to earlier, more coordinated behavioral health support directly on campus. This work continues our commitment to improving youth mental health outcomes and expanding support for students across North Carolina. https://bit.ly/4u7x3ny

  • Council for Digital Safety and Wellbeing (CDSW)

    680 followers

    1w

    "When digital screens replace human eye contact, a child's developmental milestones pay the price." We are pleased to welcome Dr. Divya Devulapally, a leading Consultant Child and Adolescent Psychiatrist, to the Board of Studies and Advisory Group at the Council for Digital Safety and Wellbeing (CDSW). Drawing from her clinical experience, Dr. Divya highlighted a pressing challenge within the pediatric healthcare ecosystem - the rising trend of "Digital Autism," where excessive screen exposure limits speech, dampens social curiosity, and isolates children from real-world human connection. Strategic Interventions for Child Development: 1. Addressing Cognitive Gaps: Developing institutional frameworks to target developmental issues arising from early childhood screen dependence. 2. Deploying Family Boundaries: Empowering parents with clear, manageable guidelines to separate necessary learning tools from passive scrolling. 3. Rebuilding Real Connection: Restoring active human communication, sensory play, and real-world exploration as the main drivers of a child's mental growth. CDSW is proud to have Dr. Divya’s clinical guidance as we shape national safety benchmarks for early childhood digital health. Dr. Anil Rachamalla Aditya Vuchi Dr Divya Devulapally #CDSW #ChildPsychiatry #DigitalWellbeing #EarlyChildhoodDevelopment

    • John T. Walkup, MD
      1w
      • Report this post

      To my distinguished colleagues at the American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry (AACAP), let us stand together in difficult times, supporting one another with compassion and respect. AACAP is our professional home, where we celebrate all of our backgrounds and perspectives, enriched by our families, cultures, faith, and lived experiences. Our diversity is our strength, and we stand united in our shared mission to serve children, adolescents, and families. In difficult times, we do what we do best: bring people together for healing, inspire hope, and give voice to the most vulnerable among us. The world feels very heavy right now. Geopolitical conflicts are rewriting daily life for children and families caught in the crosshairs. Dehumanizing policies are tearing apart the social fabric of our families and pitting communities against each other. We don’t want to live in a world where people do not feel safe in their homes, schools, communities, and places of worship, yet this is where we are. I know that you, as members of our AACAP community, understand this and feel it deeply. I am writing today because we at AACAP recognize the stress, grief, and helplessness many of you may be feeling now, especially as we continue to show up for our patients, trainees, and colleagues while also experiencing our own pain and uncertainty. But we are no strangers to adversity—and we have the power to choose how we respond in times of crisis. We choose humanity, compassion, and our commitment to one another and to the children and families we care for. At the end of the day, we are in the service of protecting the hope, promise, and future of our young people. We may not share the same experiences or perspectives, but we do share a collective humanity. I know that we will continue to come together and meet each other with empathy, respect, compassion, and care. AACAP stands strong in our collective mission to advance the mental health and well-being of children and adolescents everywhere, while ensuring that our AACAP community has the support it needs to meet the challenges ahead together.

      What began as a vision to improve access to mental healthcare has evolved into a comprehensive behavioral health organization serving children, adolescents, adults, and families across our region. With the licensure of our Pediatric Partial Hospitalization Program, Row Psychiatric Services now offers a full continuum of behavioral healthcare services, including: - Outpatient treatment - Intensive outpatient programs - Partial hospitalization - ABA services - Adult autism waiver supports We believe every individual deserves access to the right level of care at the right time. This milestone positions Row Psychiatric Services among the few organizations in our region capable of providing integrated behavioral health services across multiple levels of care and throughout the lifespan. We are proud of what we have built, grateful to our team and community partners, and excited for what lies ahead. Our commitment remains unchanged: expanding access, improving outcomes, and transforming lives. Thank you to the Chester County Department of MH/IDD, the Pennsylvania Department of Human Services, Goldman Sachs' 10,000 Businesses, SBA Thrive Emerging Leaders, Nasdaq Entrepreneurial Center, and our supporters.

      A Milestone Moment for Row Psychiatric Services We are proud to announce that Row Psychiatric Services has received licensure to operate a Pediatric Partial Hospitalization Program (PHP). Our mission has always been to eliminate barriers to care and provide services that are equitable, accessible, evidence based, and support individuals throughout every stage of their mental health journey. The addition of our Pediatric PHP strengthens our ability to serve youth who require intensive therapeutic support while remaining connected to their families, schools, and communities. This milestone reflects the dedication of our clinicians, staff, community partners, and the families who place their trust in us every day. We are honored to continue expanding access to behavioral healthcare and remain committed to building one of the region's leading behavioral health organizations.     • The Concord Center

      342 followers

      2w

      This morning's Boston Globe published findings that confirmed what we've been witnessing firsthand here at The Concord Center. A new JAMA Network Open study analyzing over 1.8 million Massachusetts children found that nearly 1 in 10 pediatric visits now includes a mental health diagnosis. Anxiety alone jumped from 1.7% to 6.1% of all pediatric visits between 2014 and 2023, the steepest climb of any diagnosis studied. Pediatric primary care has quietly become the front line of children's mental health because it's where families already are and specialized care can be too hard to access. Massachusetts continues to face a serious shortage of mental health providers, and not all services are trained in evidence-based treatments for anxiety and mood disorders. At The Concord Center, we see anxious children every week. Anxiety is often, as one expert in the article put it, "a canary in the coal mine." Early intervention works. Catching it in a pediatrician's office is a start, but it can't be the finish line. If your child or a child you know is struggling, please don't wait for the system to come to you. Reach out. Ask questions. We're here. 📍 The Concord Center | Concord, MA www.concordcenter.com Read the full article: https://lnkd.in/e6CgNpcr #ChildMentalHealth #Anxiety #PediatricCare #MassachusettsHealth #TheConcordCenter #MentalHealthAwareness #EarlyIntervention

      Today, the AAP is participating in a Mental Health Day of Action, along with several other partner organizations. Amid an ongoing national emergency in youth mental health, it is a critical time to urge lawmakers to support policies that invest in prevention and early intervention.   The focus of the Day of Action is passage of the Early Action and Responsiveness Lifts Youth (EARLY) Minds Act (H.R. 1735/S. 779).   This bipartisan, bicameral bill would provide states with more flexibility for funding key services that help young people get the care they need before a mental health condition is diagnosed or gets worse. Although the onset of most mental disorders occurs during childhood, young people too often miss out on services and interventions that could provide them with support.   Tell your lawmakers to co-sponsor and pass the EARLY Minds Act and invest in youth mental health! The Kansas Chapter, American Academy of Pediatrics (KAAP)

      Stop just asking about grades at parent-teacher conferences. You only get a few minutes face-to-face with the adult who sees a side of your child you never do. The letter grades are already on the report card, so don't waste your limited time repeating them. Instead, ask about their nervous system. Inspired by an incredible framework from creator Katy G. (https://lnkd.in/eTR_6qTk), I want you to focus on how your child handles transitions, frustration, and peer dynamics when you aren't in the room. From my chair as a pediatric psychiatric provider, a teacher's behavioral observations are the exact clinical data points I need. Children struggling with ADHD or anxiety will often hold it together all day at school, only to completely fall apart the second they get into your car. A child's ability to regulate at school tells us infinitely more about their brain than a math score ever will. I wrote a quick, actionable checklist of the exact 3 questions you should ask your child's teacher this term, plus a breakdown of how clinicians use tools like the Vanderbilt Assessment to evaluate school behavior. You can read the full guide on the Kindred Family Care blog here: https://lnkd.in/egbRAPXR If those school hours reveal that your child is severely struggling to focus or regulate, I am currently accepting new patients across Ohio for expert evaluations and conservative medication management. Disclaimer: Educational purposes only. Not medical advice. #PediatricMentalHealth #ChildPsychiatry #PMHNP #SchoolPsychology

      The new American Academy of Pediatrics Clinical Report emphasizes the need for pediatricians to collaborate with community partners . Nearly 20% of children and adolescents in the US have a mental, emotional, developmental, or behavioral disorder. Community partners are vital resources for these youth and their families. If you work in a pediatric care setting, how are you and your team addressing this new guidance?

      American Academy of Pediatrics

      128,392 followers

      1mo

      May is Mental Health Awareness Month, and AAP has released a new clinical report with guidance for pediatricians on approaching mental health and emotional development. The goals of #MentalHealthAwarenessMonth are to reduce associated stigma, provide education and encourage access to care. The AAP report outlines how pediatricians can support families by taking a broad approach to a child’s emotional health rather than waiting until challenges arise or diagnosing and treating discrete conditions or disorders only. The clinical report provides a stepped approach that may be helpful for addressing mental and emotional health concerns in pediatrics and acknowledges that advocacy is needed to expand mental and emotional resources widely. Learn more about the report: https://bit.ly/4urxCsm
